sections.title: Passive Voice with -이/히/리/기
sections.explanation: Korean forms the passive voice by adding one of four suffixes to the verb stem: -이, -히, -리, or -기. The choice of suffix depends on the verb, and there is no universal rule — they must be memorized. Some patterns help: -이 often attaches to stems ending in ㅎ, ㄱ, or a vowel (보다 → 보이다, 쌓다 → 쌓이다, 놓다 → 놓이다). -히 often attaches to stems ending in ㅂ, ㄱ, or ㄷ (먹다 → 먹히다, 잡다 → 잡히다, 닫다 → 닫히다). -리 often attaches to stems ending in ㄹ (듣다 → 들리다, 열다 → 열리다). -기 often attaches to stems ending in ㄴ, ㅁ, or ㅅ (안다 → 안기다, 담다 → 담기다). The passive sentence structure shifts the object to the subject: 고양이가 쥐를 잡았다 (The cat caught the mouse) → 쥐가 고양이에게 잡혔다 (The mouse was caught by the cat).
sections.tip: Not every Korean verb has a lexical passive form with -이/히/리/기. For verbs that lack one, Korean uses the construction -아/어지다 instead (e.g., 만들다 → 만들어지다). When the lexical passive exists, it is generally preferred over -아/어지다.

examples.en: The door is opened (열다 → 열리다, passive with -리)
The sound is heard (듣다 → 들리다, passive with -리)
The thief is caught (잡다 → 잡히다, passive with -히)
The mountain is visible (보다 → 보이다, passive with -이)

exercises.prompt: What is the passive form of 먹다 (to eat)?
exercises.explanation: 먹다 takes the -히 passive suffix: 먹히다 (to be eaten). Example: 사과가 먹혔다 — The apple was eaten.

exercises.prompt: Which passive suffix does 안다 (to hold/embrace) take?
exercises.explanation: 안다 takes the -기 suffix to form the passive 안기다 (to be held). Stems ending in ㄴ often take -기.

options: That star is visible from here
I see that star from here
That star is looking from here
exercises.explanation: 보이다 is the passive of 보다 (to see). 보인다 means 'is seen/is visible.' The star is the subject being seen, not doing the seeing.

sections.title: Causative Forms with -이/히/리/기/우/추
sections.explanation: Korean also uses suffixes to create causative verbs — meaning 'to make/cause someone to do something.' The suffixes overlap with passives (-이, -히, -리, -기) but also include -우 and -추. Like passives, the specific suffix must be memorized per verb. Examples: 먹다 (to eat) → 먹이다 (to feed — make someone eat). 죽다 (to die) → 죽이다 (to kill — cause to die). 읽다 (to read) → 읽히다 (to make someone read). 울다 (to cry) → 울리다 (to make someone cry). 웃다 (to laugh) → 웃기다 (to make someone laugh). 자다 (to sleep) → 재우다 (to put someone to sleep). 낮다 (to be low) → 낮추다 (to lower). The causative sentence introduces a causer: 아이가 먹어요 (The child eats) → 엄마가 아이에게 먹여요 (The mother feeds the child).
sections.tip: Notice that some verb stems use the same suffix for both passive and causative — context and sentence structure determine meaning. For example, 보이다 can mean 'to be seen' (passive) or 'to show' (causative).

examples.en: The mother feeds the baby milk (먹다 → 먹이다)
That movie made me cry (울다 → 울리다)
I put the child to sleep (자다 → 재우다)
That story made everyone laugh (웃다 → 웃기다)

exercises.explanation: 먹이다 is the causative form of 먹다 (to eat). It means 'to feed' — to cause someone to eat. The passive form is 먹히다 (to be eaten).

exercises.prompt: What is the causative form of 자다 (to sleep)?
exercises.explanation: 자다 → 재우다 (to put someone to sleep). This is one of the verbs that takes the -우 causative suffix, with a stem vowel change from ㅏ to ㅐ.

exercises.prompt: In '선생님이 학생에게 책을 읽혔어요,' what does 읽히다 mean?
exercises.explanation: Here 읽히다 is used causatively: the teacher made the student read the book. The same form can be passive ('to be read') in a different sentence structure. Context determines meaning.

sections.title: Passive with -아/어지다
sections.explanation: When a verb has no lexical passive form (no -이/히/리/기 passive exists), Korean uses the auxiliary construction -아/어지다 to express a passive or change-of-state meaning. This is formed by adding 지다 to the -아/어 form of the verb. For verbs: 만들다 (to make) → 만들어지다 (to be made). 사용하다 (to use) → 사용되다 (to be used — using the Sino-Korean passive 되다). For adjectives, -아/어지다 means 'to become': 예쁘다 (to be pretty) → 예뻐지다 (to become pretty). 좋다 (to be good) → 좋아지다 (to become good/better). 날씨가 따뜻해졌어요 (The weather became warm). This pattern is very productive and widely used in everyday Korean.
sections.tip: Use -아/어지다 freely with adjectives to mean 'become [adjective].' With action verbs, use it for passive meaning only when no lexical passive (-이/히/리/기) exists. For Sino-Korean verbs (하다 verbs), the passive is often formed with 되다 instead: 발견하다 → 발견되다.

exercises.explanation: 좋아지다 means 'to become good/better' — it combines the adjective 좋다 with -아지다 to show a change of state. So 길이 좋아졌어요 = The road became better.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'to be used' from 사용하다 (to use)?
exercises.explanation: Sino-Korean verbs (하다 verbs) typically form passives with 되다, not -아/어지다. So 사용하다 → 사용되다 (to be used).

options: to be made pretty by someone
exercises.explanation: When -아/어지다 is attached to an adjective, it expresses 'to become [adjective].' 예쁘다 (to be pretty) → 예뻐지다 (to become pretty).
